description_long: This guard stone is a robust, cylindrical object with a rounded top and a distinct two-tone coloration. The upper portion is a weathered, stone-like material with a rough, irregular surface, showing naturalistic texturing and some shallow indentations. The lower section is smoother and painted in a reddish-brown hue, forming a clear boundary with the upper stone part. The transition between the two materials is uneven, suggesting the stone is set into or atop a base. The object appears solid and heavy, designed to withstand impacts and prevent vehicles from encroaching onto protected areas. Its utilitarian form and durable construction are typical of guard stones found in urban environments to mark boundaries or protect infrastructure.
description: A cylindrical guard stone with a rough, rounded stone top and a smooth, reddish-brown base, designed for impact resistance and boundary marking.
description_view_0: Front view shows the rounded stone top with visible indentations and a clear transition to the reddish-brown base.
description_view_1: Side view reveals the irregular, bulging contour of the stone and the cylindrical shape of the base.
description_view_2: Rear view highlights the smoother, less detailed back of the stone and the even curve of the base.
description_view_3: Opposite side view displays the elongated profile of the stone and the consistent cylindrical form of the base.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the edge of urban streets, near corners, driveways, or building entrances to prevent vehicle encroachment and protect infrastructure.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be installed upright with the base partially embedded in the ground for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Likely to show chipping and smoothing on the upper stone portion from vehicle contact and weathering, with possible discoloration or fading of the painted base due to foot traffic and exposure to the elements. The transition area may have accumulated grime or erosion.
